Message: ?? Hay5 – Celebrating 5 Years of HaystackAnalytics! ?? Five years ago, our founders – CEO Anirvan Chatterjee, COO Gaurav Srivastava & CSM Kiran Kondabagil – started HaystackAnalytics with a bold vision: to make genomics accessible, applicable, and affordable. Today, as we celebrate our 5th Foundation Day, we reflect on a journey built on science, innovation, and impact. From tackling infectious diseases with infexn™ NGS, FLU-NGS, FEVER-NGS, Opthal NGS, and ? TB to enabling proactive healthcare through human genomics with DGx Cardio Screening, DGx Pediatric Screening and PGx Comprehensive , we’ve strived to deliver first-line answers that empower clinicians, researchers, and patients alike. Over these five years, our mission has been clear: transforming healthcare through the power of Next-Generation Sequencing (NGS) – bringing actionable insights from genomics to real-world patient care. This milestone is not just ours – it belongs to our team, partners, and the scientific community who have trusted and supported us at every step. Here’s to continuing our journey of innovation, impact, and rewriting the future of healthcare with genomics. Abhimanyuu Nambisan Pranav Godse Shuja A. When organs start shutting down, it isn’t just a complication — it’s Sepsis. A silent killer that can turn deadly within hours. Routine tests often fail to keep up with its speed. That’s where infexn™ NGS transforms the game — a single-test solution for infectious diseases with a rapid 24-hour turnaround time. Because in Sepsis, timely diagnosis isn’t just important… it is a necessity! #PublicHealth #HealthCare #Diagnostic #Genomics Abhimanyuu Nambisan Pranav Godse siddharth sasane Simran Malik Anirvan Chatterjee Gaurav Srivastava Rohan S SALVI Shuja A. A complex infection. Multiple tests. Yet, every result came back negative. Sputum samples, upper respiratory panels, and broncho cultures all failed to provide clarity, while the patient’s condition continued to deteriorate. Dr. Siddhant Shetty shares his experience with this challenging case, where conventional diagnostics fell short. Turning to sequencing with infexn™ NGS, he was able to identify the accurate pathogen — guiding the right treatment and changing the course of the patient’s care. This case highlights the critical need for precision diagnostics and demonstrates how infexn™ NGS delivers clarity when conventional tests reach their limits Gaurav Srivastava Anirvan Chatterjee Dr Mahua Das Gupta (Kapoor) Abhimanyuu Nambisan Pranav Godse siddharth sasane Shuja A. Redefining pediatric respiratory infection care - one test at a time. Dr. Parul Shukla, Pediatrician - shares how FLU-NGS is transforming the way pediatric respiratory infections are diagnosed and managed. With painless sampling, comprehensive detection, and results delivered in just 24 hours, it gives doctors the clarity they need to make the right treatment decisions — including when antibiotics are not necessary. This not only improves clinical outcomes but also brings immense relief to parents, who gain answers without long waits or unnecessary medications. For children, it means faster recovery, gentler care, and a healthier future — all starting with a single, powerful test. Rohan S SALVI Abhimanyuu Nambisan Pranav Godse siddharth sasane Shuja A. Gitesh Srivastava Mohammad Umair Anupama Pradeepkumar Namith N Gaurav Srivastava Dr Mahua Das Gupta (Kapoor) #HealthCare #Diagnostics #Genomics #PublicHealth #PediatricCare #Pediatrician

Heart health isn’t just about lifestyle — genetics plays a big role too. While two people may follow the same lifestyle, their heart risks can differ significantly because of genetic variations. This is a perspective that deserves more attention, especially as heart concerns rise among younger generations. Dr. Aparna Bhanushali shares her expert insights on this crucial connection between genetics, lifestyle, and heart health in leading publications — Hindustan Times and Firstpost. From understanding why millennials are increasingly at risk, to exploring how personalized approaches to diet and health can make a difference — these articles shed light on how science is reshaping our understanding of heart disease. ?? Pneumonia continues to be the world’s leading cause of infectious disease for children, claiming more lives each year than malaria, HIV, and measles combined. Yet, it often begins silently — with mild symptoms that resemble a common flu. By the time it’s recognized, it can rapidly progress to respiratory distress, sepsis, or even death, especially in children under five. infexn NGS changes the game with early and precise detection. Our direct-from-sample genomic solution, enables timely and accurate diagnosis within 24 hours, identifying both the pathogen and AMR genes to guide targeted therapy. Early diagnosis doesn’t just treat pneumonia — it prevents lives from being lost too soon. The VRE outbreak at Christchurch Hospital highlights a critical reality in clinical settings — colonisation often precedes detection, and multiple strains can emerge before symptoms ever appear. Such events reinforce the need to integrate high-resolution genomic surveillance into routine IPC strategy. Culture-based methods alone may miss asymptomatic carriers and early transmission chains. A culture-free, NGS-based approach — such as infexn™ NGS — can rapidly identify Enterococci species and resistance markers like VanA/VanB within 24 hours, supporting data-driven containment and antimicrobial stewardship. Early detection is no longer early — it’s the most responsible clinical step forward. Abhimanyuu Nambisan Pranav Godse siddharth sasane Rohan S SALVI Shuja A. Familial Hypercholesterolemia (FH) is a heritable disorder where LDL levels stay elevated from birth — often without symptoms. Despite lifestyle efforts, these individuals remain at high cardiovascular risk, especially in their 30s and 40s. Why FH needs early attention: Caused by mutations in LDLR, APOB, or PCSK9 Often undiagnosed until cardiac events occur Early detection enables treatment and family screening Genetic testing helps identify at-risk individuals early, enabling timely intervention and effective risk management. Let’s shift from reactive treatment to proactive prevention. Genetic insights can change outcomes. Long QT Syndrome (LQTS) is an inherited cardiac rhythm disorder that affects the heart’s electrical activity, increasing the risk of syncope, seizures, and sudden cardiac arrest — often in individuals with no prior symptoms. Most cases are linked to pathogenic variants in genes such as KCNQ1, KCNH2, and SCN5A, which regulate cardiac ion channels and electrical signaling. Why early genetic detection matters: ? Identifies individuals at risk before clinical events occur ? Enables timely clinical management ? Facilitates cascade screening for family members Genetic testing plays a critical role in shifting care from event-driven response to preventive cardiology. Know your heart’s rhythm. High AQI is no longer just an environmental concern—it has direct implications for respiratory health and infection dynamics. Particulate exposure alters respiratory immunity across different sites. In the upper respiratory tract, inflammation and impaired mucociliary clearance can mask or mimic viral infections. In the lower respiratory tract, fine particles penetrate deeper, weakening immune defenses and increasing the risk and severity of infections. As pollution-driven inflammation increasingly blurs clinical presentation, site-specific and advanced diagnostics become essential. NGS-based tools like FLU-NGS, optimized for upper respiratory infections, and infexn® NGS, designed for complex lower respiratory infections, help align diagnostics with how respiratory disease truly presents today. Superbug Delhi: When winter air becomes a carrier of drug-resistant bacteria. Recent findings show that Delhi’s winter air contains high levels of antibiotic-resistant bacteria, adding a new layer of risk to an already strained respiratory environment. Polluted air can compromise airway defenses and disrupt early immune responses, allowing resistant pathogens to persist and establish infection. When infections emerge under these conditions, delayed pathogen identification often leads to empirical antibiotic use, prolonged illness, and increased selective pressure for resistance. infexn®-NGS demonstrates how a single, comprehensive diagnostic test can provide early clarity, identifying pathogens and resistance markers together to support timely, evidence-based clinical decisions. Timely diagnosis is fundamental to responsible antibiotic use, effective antimicrobial stewardship, and meaningful progress against AMR. Genetic Restrictive Cardiomyopathy (RCM) is a rare inherited condition that silently stiffens the heart muscle, limiting its ability to fill with blood even when pumping strength appears normal. This often leads to diastolic dysfunction without obvious cardiac enlargement. Genetic insights are critical for early risk identification. Advanced genomic tests like DGx Cardio Screening help uncover inherited cardiac predispositions early, enabling timely surveillance, family screening, and preventive interventions well before symptoms progress or rare sudden cardiac events emerge. Because in inherited cardiac conditions, early knowledge can change outcomes and one genetic diagnosis can help protect an entire family. Are we losing our frontline antibiotics? Once a trusted first-line therapy, ciprofloxacin is now showing alarming resistance across major bacterial pathogens, as highlighted by a large clinical study from SMS Hospital Jaipur. This growing resistance reflects a deeper challenge, treating infections without timely, accurate pathogen identification. Antimicrobial Resistance (AMR) is no longer a future concern; it is a present clinical reality driven by empirical therapy, delayed diagnostics, and limited visibility into the true causative organism. The consequence is slower recovery, increased testing, and unpredictable treatment outcomes. This is where infexn® NGS adds critical clinical value. By enabling early, comprehensive pathogen detection along with antimicrobial resistance gene (ARG) insights, infexn® NGS supports evidence-based antibiotic selection, moving care away from assumptions toward precision-driven decisions. The Spanish Flu didn’t act alone. It was the unseen secondary infections that turned a viral outbreak into a global catastrophe. A century ago, millions died not just from the virus, but from bacterial pneumonia that followed lung damage, when treatment was guided by symptoms, not by the causative pathogen making clinical outcomes uncertain. Today, the story hasn’t changed, only the setting has. Overlapping infections, blurred symptoms, inflamed lungs, and delayed pathogen identification continue to shape clinical decisions, often leading to empirical treatment and missed co-infections. infexn®-NGS redefines this gap by delivering comprehensive detection of viruses, bacteria, and co-infections from a single sample, offering clarity before conditions escalate. Heart attack and heart failure are frequently confused — but they represent distinct clinical conditions. A heart attack is an acute event caused by sudden interruption of blood flow to the heart muscle. Heart failure, in contrast, is typically a progressive syndrome in which the heart gradually loses its ability to pump or fill efficiently. In its early stages, heart failure may not present dramatically. Symptoms such as breathlessness on exertion, fatigue, or reduced exercise tolerance are often normalized — attributed to stress, lifestyle, or ageing rather than underlying cardiac dysfunction. For individuals with inherited cardiac predispositions, genetic insights can add another layer to risk assessment. Advanced solutions like DGx Cardio Screening support early risk stratification and informed monitoring for proactive care. This Heart Awareness Week, the focus should extend beyond acute events to recognizing silent progression and identifying risk earlier. Abhimanyuu Nambisan Pranav Godse Shuja A. What looks like survival… might actually be borrowed biology. In a controlled experiment, one rotifer species was destroyed by infection, while another shut down, then reactivated and resumed activity. The difference wasn’t chance. Some organisms can integrate bacterial genes into their own genome, using them to produce antimicrobial compounds and survive infection. What microbes use to compete… can become a defense in another system. Your cholesterol levels looked normal. That should have been enough—right? But cardiovascular risk doesn’t always align with what routine tests capture. Lp(a) is a genetically determined risk factor that often goes unmeasured, yet is strongly associated with conditions such as coronary artery disease and stroke. Even when standard lipid profiles appear normal, underlying genetic predispositions may still exist. This is where advanced genomic approaches, such as Whole Exome Sequencing (WES), can provide deeper insights—helping identify inherited risk factors, enable early risk stratification, and support more informed clinical decision-making. Because understanding risk today requires looking beyond conventional markers—into the genome itself. ??
